The ticket TI-1623017 was opened on behalf of Customer Company 60 AG and documents issues experienced by that organization's Finance users.
Contact Person 18 reported the issue and acted as the customer contact for the ticket communications and validation of the fix.

Consulting Sample Company executed the remediation steps and authored the closure communication recorded in the ticket.
The consulting organization delivered the remediation according to the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard, validated the fix with multiple users, and formally closed the ticket.

The incident's symptom was that affected users' default printer automatically switched to MFP-ZUERICH-01 (incorrect device for Basel users); ticket troubleshooting and remediation were directed to stop this behavior.
MFP-BASEL-01 is the correct location printer that should be the default for affected users; tests after remediation confirmed it remained set as default.
The ticket's closure and remediation actions were performed according to the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard; the standard defined the expected provisioning behavior and remediation steps.
The group of affected users are the subject of support ticket TI-1623017 — they reported and validated the default-printer behavior and participated in testing after remediation.
Employee 23 executed gpupdate /force (and user re-login) as part of testing to force Group Policy refresh so the corrected group membership and resulting printer assignment would take effect.

Employee 23 is the named ticket owner and performing engineer for the consulting organization that provided the service; they coordinated remediation with the customer and closed the ticket under the consulting company's banner.

The affected workstation was accessed remotely by Employee 23 during initial analysis and testing (temporary removal of group membership, gpupdate /force, re-login) to confirm the default printer behaviour.

Group Policy configuration mapped the Zurich default printer setting to MFP-ZUERICH-01 for accounts in the Zurich location group, resulting in that device becoming default where it should not have been.
Because the new Finance AD group was a member of the Zurich location group, the Zurich printer (MFP-ZUERICH-01) was applied to Finance users via GPO, causing the incorrect default setting.
MFP-ZUERICH-01 is the default printer intended for users at the Zurich location; Zurich-targeted GPOs map this device as default for the Zurich location group.
Group Policy is used to set the Basel default printer (MFP-BASEL-01) for users in the Basel location group; after AD group cleanup and policy refresh the Basel default applied correctly.
After forcing a policy refresh with gpupdate /force and re-login, the user's default printer was observed to be MFP-BASEL-01 and to remain stable, demonstrating the policy change took effect.
MFP-BASEL-01 is the default printer intended for users at the Basel location per the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and targeted GPOs.

The blueprint defines that default printers per location are set via GPO and that printer assignments are location-based via AD groups; the GPO configuration and remediation were validated against this standard.

Printer provisioning behavior (location-based AD groups, default printers via GPO, prohibition of parallel groups) is defined by the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and constitutes the procedural basis for remediation.
The Finance department was moved into a newly created AD group during reorganization; this group assignment is the starting point for the misconfiguration that caused incorrect printer mapping.
The affected users are employees of the Finance department; their AD group memberships (Finance group) determine which location/printer policies apply to them.

The new Finance AD group was (incorrectly) a member of a higher-level Zurich location AD group, causing Zurich-location GPO mappings (including the Zurich default printer) to apply to the Finance users.
The NEW FINANCE AD GROUP was (incorrectly) a member of the higher-level ZURICH LOCATION AD GROUP, causing Zurich-targeted GPOs (including default printer mappings) to apply to Finance users.
The NEW FINANCE AD GROUP is an Active Directory group object; AD houses the group and its nested memberships which determined GPO application and printer mappings.
The OLD FINANCE AD GROUP was deactivated as users were moved into the NEW FINANCE AD GROUP during reorganization; this change event led to unexpected nested membership in the Zurich location group.
The Zurich location AD group is targeted by GPOs that map and set default printers for Zurich; membership inheritance caused those GPOs to apply to the Finance group.
The Zurich location AD group represents the Zurich site/scope in Active Directory and is associated with Zurich-targeted GPOs and Zurich printer assignment (MFP-ZUERICH-01).

GROUP POLICY (GPO) and PRINTER PROVISIONING: Printer provisioning is implemented through Group Policy Objects that map print queues hosted on the print server to Active Directory groups and that set default printers by location. GPO-targeted assignments are configured so queues provisioned on the print server are automatically mapped to user devices based on location or department, ensuring users receive the appropriate printers and defaults. When provisioning issues occur, remediation involves correcting the GPO targeting/scope (so the right AD groups, locations or departments are in scope) and refreshing Group Policy on the affected machines so the updated printer mappings and default settings apply.
GPOs target AD groups/OU membership (hosted in Active Directory) to apply printer mappings and defaults; the misapplied membership in AD caused the wrong GPO (Zurich mapping) to affect Basel users.
The gpupdate /force command was executed on the affected workstation to force Group Policy refresh so the corrected AD membership and resulting printer assignment would take effect and be verified.
Ticket TI-1623488 was opened on behalf of Customer Company 60 AG and concerns an end user at that organization.
Contact Person 20 reported the incident and is the customer liaison referenced in the ticket activities.
Employee 27 was the assigned owner of the ticket and performed investigation and remediation actions leading to closure.

Consulting Sample Company executed the remediation actions, validated printing with the customer, and issued the ticket closure confirming the issue was resolved.
Consulting Sample Company performed the troubleshooting and remediation and authored the closure message on the ticket.
The ticket documents a printing failure experienced by the Affected User in the Basel office; the user's failed prints are the subject of the incident.
The Basel Office is a physical location/branch of Customer Company 60 AG where the affected user and printer MFP-BASEL-01 are located; the incident was reported for a user in this office.
Contact Person 20 reported the affected user's inability to print and coordinated the support call that generated the ticket.
Employee 27 removed the explicit Deny ACE from the printer queue during troubleshooting, after which the user logged back in and print tests succeeded.
Employee 27 referenced and followed the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard during analysis and remediation (permission cleanup), as recorded in the ticket activities and closure note.
Employee 27 established a remote connection to the User Workstation to perform investigation and remediation (reviewed the visible queue and removed the explicit Deny ACE).
Employee 27 inspected and modified settings on the Print Server (reviewed the MFP-BASEL-01 queue ACL and removed the explicit Deny ACE) as part of the remediation steps.
The Affected User attempted to print to MFP-BASEL-01 and received immediate "Access denied" errors when sending print jobs to that device/queue.
The explicit Deny entry targeted the Affected User account, overriding group-based allow permissions and preventing print jobs from being accepted/queued for that user.
The Affected User operates from the User Workstation where the remote support session was established and from which the failing print jobs were sent to MFP-BASEL-01.

MFP-BASEL-01 is a networked multifunction printer whose print queue is hosted on the PRINT SERVER. Print jobs are submitted to the PRINT SERVER and flow from the PRINT SERVER to MFP-BASEL-01. The PRINT SERVER enforces the queue's permissions and is the locus for reviewing and modifying the queue's ACEs (access control entries). During troubleshooting it was found the PRINT SERVER could not reach MFP-BASEL-01 (ping failed) due to network/firewall rules; correcting those rules restored connectivity and normal job delivery.
Printing from the Colleague Workstation to MFP-BASEL-01 succeeded, demonstrating that the printer and queue were functional and that the issue was specific to the affected user's permissions.
MFP-BASEL-01 is physically located in the Basel Office and serves users in that office as their local multifunction printer/queue.
The print server contains and manages the logical printer queue for MFP-BASEL-01, including NTFS/share and ACE entries controlling print access.

NTFS and share permission consistency is required for correct print service operation and is a consideration on the Print Server when configuring access to printers and related resources; the Standard mandates this consistency and the print server was reviewed with these constraints in mind.
The AD group "PRT-Basel" is granted print permission on the MFP-BASEL-01 queue per the permission model; the affected user was a member of this group.

An explicit Deny ACE was present on the printer queue for the affected user (originating from a prior test configuration) and directly caused the "Access denied" errors; removing this ACE restored printing.

The Standard defines how permissions on printer queues must be configured (AD group-based, consistent NTFS/share permissions); the queue's permissions were reviewed and corrected according to this document.

The Standard requires that NTFS and share permissions be consistent with queue-level access controls; the print server/queue configuration was evaluated with these requirements in mind during remediation.
The print server evaluates Active Directory group memberships (e.g., PRT-Basel) when applying queue permissions; AD is the authoritative identity/source for group-based access control on the queue.

The Printer ACL is the set of permissions configured on the MFP-BASEL-01 queue on the print server; it contained both the AD group Allow ACE and the problematic explicit Deny ACE that blocked the user.

Queue-level permissions are the specific permission settings applied to the MFP-BASEL-01 queue (the ACL/ACE composition); the incident involved correcting these queue-level permissions to align with the standard (remove individual Deny ACEs).
PRT-Basel is an AD security group stored in Active Directory; AD defines group membership that the print server relies on to grant group-based print permissions.
The AD group PRT-Basel represents users of the Basel Office (location-based group) and is used to assign printer access for users within that office.
The Explicit Deny ACE is an entry within the Printer ACL for the MFP-BASEL-01 queue; its presence caused immediate "Access denied" for the affected user's print attempts.

Ticket TI-1602241 documents an incident affecting MICROSOFT TEAMS. The issue involved external PSTN calls placed via Direct Routing where audio from the external participants was not heard by local (internal) Teams users (inbound/one-way audio loss). Microsoft Teams diagnostics were used during the analysis of the problem.
The impacted users (about a dozen) were all at the Zurich location; the problematic firewall rule was applied at this location and thus implicated in the incident.
The ticket investigation identified the Zurich firewall rule change as the root cause (media ports restricted), and the ticket was resolved by correcting this configuration.
The incident was escalated to the Unified Communications / Network team for investigation and remediation of Direct Routing/media-flow and firewall configuration.
Different headset models were present among affected users (Jabra, Poly), which supported the conclusion that headsets were not the common root cause for the missing audio.

For TI-1602241, CONSULTING SAMPLE COMPANY executed remediation and support activities, performed and supports the investigation, implemented a configuration change per architecture guidance, authored and signed the closure communication, and communicated the resolution to Contact Person 14 and the customer.
The firewall change is the operational event correlated with the incident reported in TI-1602241 and was identified as the root cause of one-way/absent audio for external calls.
Contact Person 14 reported that all affected employees were at the Zurich location, establishing the geographic/scope of the incident.
Employee 33 escalated/took steps that led to handing the ticket over to System Engineer 18 for advanced Direct Routing/media-flow analysis.

Zurich was specifically noted to have Media Bypass enabled; this site-level setting influenced how media must be routed and which ports the firewall needs to allow for Direct Routing to function correctly.
The Unified Communications / Network team investigated SBC behaviour (showed successful SIP, RTP one-directional) as part of analyzing the Direct Routing/media flow.
The SBC handled SIP signaling; logs showed successful SIP connections and no call drops, indicating signaling functioned correctly.

The restrictive firewall rule at Zurich partially blocked media flow for Direct Routing, causing the observed absence of inbound audio to local users during external calls.
The firewall rule was corrected according to the Direct Routing architecture/blueprint; after the adjustment voice transmission was reported stable and the ticket was closed.
The security-hardening firewall change implemented at the Zurich site resulted in the new restrictive outbound rule (the ZURICH FIREWALL RULE) that blocked required media ports.
The outbound traffic rule is the concrete manifestation of the Zurich firewall configuration that was found to be more restrictive (described in ticket analysis and firewall comparison).

Microsoft documentation was used to compare and validate the correct media port ranges; this comparison showed the Zurich rule deviated from Microsoft guidance and internal specification.
RTP media uses the defined media port ranges; blocking or restricting these ranges on the firewall prevents bidirectional RTP and causes one-way audio issues.
The outbound traffic rule at Zurich did not permit the full media port ranges required for RTP as defined by Microsoft/internal blueprint, directly causing blocked RTP streams.

Microsoft's guidance was used to determine the authoritative media port ranges required for RTP/Direct Routing and to validate the correct configuration against the Zurich firewall rule and internal blueprint.
Microsoft Teams external calls rely on correct media flow (RTP) via Direct Routing; when media flow is blocked, Teams users experience missing audio despite calls being connected.
Temporarily opening the media ports allowed media flow to function bidirectionally and restored audio during tests, demonstrating the firewall rule was the cause; when reverted, the issue recurred.
SDP negotiation established media parameters, but subsequent RTP traffic was blocked or restricted by the firewall; SDP itself succeeded but RTP failed in one direction.

The incident documented in TI-1624175 concerns the multifunction device (scan-to-mail) at the Zurich location whose external deliveries were failing.
Resolution of the ticket required changing SMTP relay configuration (relay allow list and DNS behavior); the closure explicitly notes SMTP relay adjustments per the standard.
Troubleshooting and remediation were performed according to this blueprint; closure references following the standard to restore Scan-to-Mail.
Consulting Sample Company performed the investigation and applied configuration changes, then validated and closed the ticket.
Contact Person 25 reported that scanning from the multifunction device at Zurich delivered internally but not to external recipients, initiating the troubleshooting workflow.
Contact Person 25 reported that the external mail security solution had been adjusted the previous week, providing context and a potential contributing change to the mail delivery behavior.

Contact Person 25 reported that new DNS forwarding was configured at the location, which investigators reviewed as part of the incident analysis in relation to the Print & Scanning blueprint requirement for internal DNS resolution.
Employee 30 (ticket owner) performed or initiated the mail-log analysis that observed "Relay denied" entries in the SMTP logs during troubleshooting.
Employee 30 executed test scans (to internal and external addresses) and validated delivery behavior from the multifunction device during investigation and the test phase.
Consulting Sample Company executed or coordinated the SMTP relay configuration changes (per the blueprint) and validated functionality during test phase.
The multifunction device's IP address is in the newly defined printer VLAN; this VLAN's address range was not previously allowed by the SMTP relay, causing the external delivery failure.

SMTP logs recorded "Relay denied" for external delivery attempts originating from the multifunction device, linking the device's failed deliveries to the relay's refusal to forward messages.
Log entries showing "Relay denied" were tied to delivery attempts originating from the multifunction device's IP, providing direct diagnostic evidence of the root cause.
Test scans from the multifunction device were delivered successfully to internal mailboxes (internal delivery), showing a direct sending path to internal mail recipients.
The multifunction device uses the site's internal routing to reach internal mail services; the notes state internal routing works, enabling successful internal deliveries.
The SMTP relay enforces an allow list of IP ranges; the printer VLAN's IP range was not included and thus the relay denied forwarding mail from that VLAN until the configuration was updated.
The SMTP relay produced log entries (e.g., "Relay denied") which were analyzed to identify the cause of external delivery failures and to confirm the relay's behavior toward the printer's IP range.
The blueprint mandates that printers must send via a dedicated SMTP relay and that the relay must allow only defined IP ranges; remediation aligned the relay configuration with these requirements.

The MAIL TEAM manages and configures the SMTP RELAY. They were engaged to update the SMTP RELAY configuration to whitelist the printer VLAN IP range so devices on that VLAN can relay mail, and to validate that the relay forwarded external mail after the change. Separately, the MAIL TEAM had previously reduced the SMTP RELAY's maximum email size and, during coordination with the support team, later adjusted that relay size limit to meet operational requirements. In short, the MAIL TEAM is responsible for both access controls (IP whitelisting) and message-size policies on the SMTP RELAY and performs validation and coordination with support teams after changes.
Successful test deliveries to a Gmail address after the relay configuration change demonstrate that the SMTP relay is now forwarding external mail to consumer providers.
Successful test deliveries to a partner company's mailbox after remediation demonstrate external corporate delivery is functioning through the relay.

Internal deliveries occurred directly to internal mailboxes (bypassing the SMTP relay for local delivery), while external deliveries required forwarding via the SMTP relay which was denying relay from the printer VLAN until updated.
The blueprint requires DNS resolution to occur internally for Scan-to-Mail workflows; investigators reviewed local DNS forwarding changes to ensure compliance with the standard.
Network Team confirmed the configuration of the new printer VLAN and coordinated to have its IP range added to the SMTP relay allow list.

Customer reported recent DNS forwarding changes and an adjustment to the external mail security solution; these recent changes were investigated as potential contributing factors to mail delivery behavior.
Network and Mail teams coordinated to confirm the printer VLAN was not included in the SMTP relay allow list and to implement the relay configuration changes that restored external delivery.

source: PRINT SERVICES FOR SAP OUTPUT STANDARD VERSION 1.0
description: The standard references ticket TI-931293 as a concrete example motivating the documented procedures (post-update/restore validation and queue property standardization) and indicates that the decision tree and operational checks address the pattern observed in that ticket.

SAP print paths within Customer Company 13 are declared business-critical by the standard and must be prioritized (PRIO HIGH) when disrupted; the company is the owner/operational boundary for those paths.
TI-931293 is cited as an example ticket where printing issues occurred after a Windows update and subsequent VM restore, linking the ticket to VM restore-related queue problems.
The symptoms documented in TI-931293 (stuck jobs, disappearing jobs, accumulation on printserver) are attributed to print queue and publishing/configuration deviations described in the standard.
The example ticket TI-931293 described jobs remaining stuck in SAP and other spool-related symptoms, implicating the SAP-side spool queue as part of the root-cause investigation.
The SAP System uses SAP Spool / Output Management to generate and manage print jobs; this is the source of SAP-originating print data.

SAP-side errors are generated by the SAP System/SAP Spool subsystem and indicate problems with print target definitions, spool transfer, or SAP configuration when multiple printservers are implicated.
SAP Spool transfers jobs to defined SAP Print Targets (logical printers) which map to the Windows side for actual printing.
The spool queue is the SAP-side buffer/queue managed by SAP Spool/Output Management that holds jobs before they are transferred to SAP print targets and onward to Windows printservers.
SAP print targets map to and send jobs to Windows Printservers (dedicated SAP Printservers) where corresponding queues receive and process the jobs.
VRINFRGERPRNT03 is provided as an example/instance of a Windows Printserver (SAP Printserver) described in the standard and hosting SAP-related print queues.
VRINFRGERPRNT04 is provided as a second example/instance of a Windows Printserver (SAP Printserver) described in the standard and hosting SAP-related print queues.
Print queues are hosted on the Windows Printservers; their configuration, publishing and status are managed on these servers and are primary validation checkpoints in the standard.
Windows updates on printserver VMs are identified as triggers that require validation of the printserver, queues, spooler and publishing; the standard prescribes post-update validation steps.
Jobs flow from the SAP spool queue to the Windows Printserver queues; visibility or transfer failures between these components can cause jobs to remain stuck in SAP.

Windows Printservers must have network connectivity to target printers; the document instructs checking network path/printer reachability when spooler and queues appear healthy but no printing occurs.
A test print from SAP validates end-to-end processing on the Windows Printserver (queue acceptance, spooler handling, and forwarding to physical printer) and is recommended after updates/restores.
Print queues map to physical printers; successful queue processing on the printserver is required to reach the physical device and produce output.
Queue Properties define how each print queue behaves and is published/shared; the document requires these properties to be standardized and validated to prevent SAP printing failures.

Sharing and publishing mechanisms determine whether SAP and clients can see and send jobs to print queues; broken or inconsistent publishing after updates/restores can cause print jobs to be stuck or lost.

VM restore events are explicitly called out as a common cause of inconsistent queue states (publishing/sharing parameters reset or incorrect) and thus require post-restore validation of queues per the standard.

Publishing/sharing parameters determine whether print queues are visible and addressable by SAP and clients; misconfiguration here is called out as a common cause of printing issues after updates/restores.

Queue status is the observed state attribute of print queues (paused, jobs queued, job states) and is a primary validation checkpoint; inconsistent status indicates configuration or operational problems.

Misconfigured queue properties can cause spooler behavior problems (stuck jobs, jobs disappearing) and therefore queue properties are treated as a primary checkpoint beyond simply restarting the spooler.
Network path and reachability determine whether the Windows Printserver can successfully deliver print jobs to the physical printers; network issues can result in visible queues but no output.
Queue status and spooler operation are related diagnostics: the spooler processes queue jobs, but correct queue status is an independent checkpoint beyond simply restarting the spooler.
Publishing/sharing parameters are the configurable settings implemented via network sharing mechanisms; they are operationalized by those mechanisms and must be validated together.

description: Carrier engagement and feedback were part of the ticket investigation: carrier confirmed calls were delivered to the SBC, helping to narrow root cause to SBC-side translation/routing configuration.

source: BLUEPRINT – MICROSOFT TEAMS DIRECT ROUTING & NUMBER TRANSLATION – VERSION 2.3
description: The Blueprint documents the Direct Routing architecture, including translation/normalization and voice routing guidance used to correctly configure number handling between carrier, SBC, and Teams; it is the authoritative reference for Direct Routing configuration.

source: BLUEPRINT – MICROSOFT TEAMS DIRECT ROUTING & NUMBER TRANSLATION – VERSION 2.3
description: The internal documentation differed from or had not been synchronized with the official Direct Routing Blueprint; the team compared actual SBC configuration to the Blueprint during analysis and used the Blueprint as the authoritative spec for the permanent change.

Teams Admin Center is the administrative interface used to monitor Teams inbound call events and diagnostics; absence of incoming call entries for affected numbers in the Admin Center corroborated that calls were not reaching Teams.
Carrier delivers PSTN calls to the customer's SBC; carrier confirmed calls reached the SBC and saw no upstream delivery issues, which helped isolate the failure to SBC-side processing.
The new number range was assigned/provisioned by the carrier; the carrier and customer migration of that range triggered the need to update SBC translation rules.

The SBC translation table is the concrete configuration implementing the routing and translation rules (number normalization, mapping of incoming ranges). New numbers were missing from these tables/rules causing misrouting.

The new number range was missing from the SBC translation table, causing INVITEs for that range to be mis-answered and not forwarded to Teams; adding the range to the translation table resolved the issue.
Translation rules normalize incoming numbers and feed into voice routing policies which determine how calls are delivered to Teams; these components work together as specified in the Blueprint.
Routing and translation rules implement E.164 normalization to convert provider number formats into the canonical form required by voice routing and internal SIP routing.
Log findings (INVITE accepted vs INVITE with 404) indicated that routing/translation rules on the SBC did not correctly handle the new number range, guiding the team to update these rules.

Routing/translation rules on the SBC did not include the new number range (they covered only the old range), so normalization/translation failed for calls from the new range until rules were extended.
The existing routing/translation rules covered the old number range and correctly normalized and routed those calls to Teams; this contrast helped isolate the missing entries for the new range.

SBC produced logs analyzed by the support team; logs showed differing SIP responses (INVITE accepted and forwarded for working numbers vs INVITE answered with 404 Not Found for affected numbers), which localized the fault to SBC configuration/translation.

SBC logs showed that when the temporary translation rule was applied, INVITEs for the previously affected numbers were accepted and forwarded (log behavior changed from prior "404 Not Found"), confirming the rule fixed the translation/routing issue during testing.

Direct Routing is implemented via the customer's SBC which receives calls from the carrier and applies translation/routing rules before forwarding to Teams; the SBC is the on-premises component of the Direct Routing architecture.
The carrier delivered incoming PSTN/SIP calls to the customer's SBC over the SIP trunk; delivery to the SIP trunk/SBC was confirmed by the carrier, which meant the transport link was functioning.

The temporary translation rule was applied on the SBC to add the new number range for testing; with the temporary rule active, test calls for the new range were correctly signaled and delivered to Teams.
The support ticket TI-1609823 was opened on behalf of Customer Company 52 AG and documents the outage affecting that customer's users.
Contact Person 21 reported the issue by email and received communications about status and closure; they are the ticket contact.
Employee 39 was the assigned owner/engineer for the ticket and led analysis, tests, coordination, and final remediation actions.

Consulting Sample Company performed the work, authored communications, and closed the ticket on behalf of the support organization.
Consulting Sample Company carried out analysis, tests, policy adjustments according to the Teams Voice Architecture/blueprint, and communicated closure to the customer.
The incident concerns Microsoft Teams voice functionality (external calling failure) and the Teams voice configuration was the primary investigation focus.
Teams Admin Center was used to analyze user license and voice routing settings (identified Direct Routing, licensing state, and which voice routing policy each user had assigned).

The provisioning script's behavior (assigning Global by default) was identified as the root cause of the service degradation documented in the ticket; correcting the script/policy mapping was part of the remediation recorded in the ticket.
Helpdesk performed the initial inquiry and triage for ticket TI-1609823 (asked diagnostic questions and gathered scope/details before escalation).

The ticket's remediation and closing note referenced adjustments made "according to Teams Voice Architecture"; the architecture doc guided the routing configuration changes that resolved external calling.
Helpdesk communicated with Contact Person 21 to collect information about affected users, scope, and recent changes (early customer interaction).

MICROSOFT TEAMS — Direct Routing: The customer's Microsoft Teams telephony integration uses Direct Routing (not Microsoft Calling Plan) to connect Teams to the PSTN via a Session Border Controller (SBC). Direct Routing was the integration mode implicated in the incident. Because this deployment relies on Direct Routing, correct configuration of voice routing policies and PSTN Usages is required for inbound and outbound call routing through the SBC; misconfiguration of those elements can prevent proper PSTN connectivity.
Calling Plan was evaluated as part of the investigation and was reported as not active for the affected users (indicating Direct Routing is the active PSTN mechanism).

The affected users were configured to use Direct Routing for PSTN connectivity (as identified during Teams Admin Center analysis), making correct voice routing policy assignment critical for external calling.

The provisioning script was assigning the "Global" voice routing policy by default to users who became members of the Enterprise-Sales group; this assignment was the misconfiguration that prevented external routing.

Affected users were assigned the "Global" policy, which lacked PSTN Usage entries and therefore provided no external PSTN route for Direct Routing-enabled users, causing external calls to terminate immediately.

The "Global" policy was observed not to reference any PSTN Usage (including External-Route), meaning it did not provide outbound PSTN connectivity — a direct reason external calls failed for users assigned to it.
DR-Standard includes the PSTN Usage "External-Route", which provides the route to the PSTN and enables external dialing for users assigned to that policy.

target: BLUEPRINT – TEAMS VOICE ROUTING & POLICY ASSIGNMENT – VERSION 1.3
description: The internal blueprint documents the expected voice routing and policy assignment approach; it indicates that Direct Routing users must be assigned a dedicated routing policy (e.g., DR-Standard/DR-Sales) rather than a global/default policy.
Temporarily assigning DR-Standard (which includes the External-Route PSTN Usage) to an affected user restored external calling in tests, proving that correct policy assignment resolves the issue.
The affected users are members of the Sales team and were organizationally reassigned to the new "Enterprise Sales" department, which is central to the cause analysis.
Affected Sales users were placed into the new Azure AD group "Enterprise-Sales" during the organizational change; group membership triggered automated provisioning behavior.
The Enterprise-Sales object is an Azure AD group; Azure AD stores and manages the group and its membership which drives downstream provisioning logic.

The provisioning script watches group membership (e.g., Enterprise-Sales) and, when users are added, assigns voice routing policies automatically; a recent change caused it to assign "Global" by default for this group.
The Enterprise-Sales Azure AD group was created to represent the Enterprise Sales department; group membership maps to the department and drives provisioning behavior.
Azure AD Groups are objects within Azure AD; the new groups referenced in the ticket were created and managed in Azure AD and control membership/provisioning.

target: BLUEPRINT – TEAMS VOICE ROUTING & POLICY ASSIGNMENT – VERSION 1.3
description: The provisioning logic/configuration did not reflect the blueprint (new group structure was not reflected in the routing blueprint), prompting changes to the provisioning script to enforce correct policy assignment per the blueprint.

Coordination occurred with the Identity team to review and correct the provisioning script/logic (script had been modified two weeks earlier; Identity team collaborated to adjust provisioning logic to align with voice routing architecture).

The automated provisioning script consumes Azure AD group membership (e.g., Enterprise-Sales) to assign voice routing policies; a change in the script caused incorrect default assignment to the "Global" policy.

The provisioning logic affected users in the Enterprise Sales department by assigning a default voice routing policy when users were added to the department's Azure AD group; provisioning logic changes therefore directly impacted the department's telephony behavior.

AD FS can issue JWT tokens via OpenID Connect/OAuth2 client registrations; the blueprint highlights token format selection and its impact on target applications.
AD FS can issue SAML tokens as an alternative to JWT; the blueprint instructs choosing SAML or JWT per application and checking SAML configuration when SAML-based apps fail.
AD FS can integrate MFA directly or via delegation; the blueprint describes options and the impact of MFA on authentication flows.
AD FS may delegate authentication or MFA to claims providers; the blueprint warns that delegating MFA/claims increases flow complexity and can cause failures if not supported by target applications.

description: Client registration parameters are recorded/configured in AD FS; the blueprint emphasizes verifying Client ID, Redirect URI and related client flags in AD FS when diagnosing integration failures.

AD FS uses certificates and signing keys to sign tokens; certificate validity and signature verification are essential for successful token validation by target applications, and the blueprint calls out certificate checks in troubleshooting.
Logging and monitoring systems should capture AD FS events, token issuance, and errors to enable diagnosis of complex authentication flows and MFA delegations as recommended by the blueprint.
AD FS is an on-premises service that runs on Windows Server; the blueprint requires/assumes a Windows Server platform to host AD FS and related services.
Conditional Access is a Microsoft Entra ID capability; availability and feature set depend on Entra ID licensing tier (P1/P2) as described in the blueprint.

SAML-consuming applications must be configured correctly and tested; when chosen, the blueprint suggests reviewing SAML configuration for failing apps.
Claims providers are often the external systems that perform MFA when AD FS is configured to forward MFA requests; this relationship affects how MFA is executed and audited.

When MFA is delegated to claims providers, logging/monitoring across both AD FS and the claims providers is necessary to trace the authentication flow and audit MFA events; the blueprint highlights this need.

The blueprint contrasts browser-based interactive flows with non-browser service/API flows, noting that browser sign-ins may succeed while non-browser integrations fail due to different client parameters, token formats, or MFA delegation.

Non-browser logins are the authentication flows used by these applications/integrations/APIs; failures in these flows point investigators to client registration and token format issues described in the blueprint.

Token signature verification and certificate validity are common causes of intermittent or user-specific failures in non-browser integrations; blueprint recommends checking certificates and time skew for these flows.

Employee 14 reported the connectivity issues affecting the TRAIN CE server and confirmed that restarting the application server did not resolve the problem.

EMPLOYEE 21 authored or contributed to the ADFS CONFIGURATION DOCUMENTATION and the related analysis, explicitly stating "We have documented the configuration." This indicates Employee 21 was directly involved in creating or updating the ADFS configuration documentation to capture configuration details and corrective actions, with the stated purpose of preventing recurrence. Their statement implies a level of responsibility for ensuring the ADFS configuration is recorded and retained for future reference.
Employee 21 addressed the detailed analysis communication to Employee 7, indicating a stakeholder notification/communication relationship.

Portal notes identify the KF11\\001-svc-axavaobt-tra service account as the account currently configured for the integration, implying this service account is used by components interacting with the TRAIN CE server and relevant integrations.
The TRAIN CE server is the specific application server referenced; a restart of the application server component was attempted as a remediation step documented in the ticket.

The Train system's authentication relies on ADFS; misconfigured ADFS client registration parameters caused authentication/connectivity failures for the Train environment until parameters were adjusted.
The TRAIN-CE/FO integration is the integration context that involves the TRAIN CE server; the connectivity/authentication issue occurred within this integration context.

The Toolbox client on the Train system uses an ADFS client registration for MFA requests; the new client had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set true, which led to the observed authentication issues.
If ADFS issues JWTs to the Toolbox client, the Toolbox must support and validate JWTs; inability to validate JWTs would cause authentication failures as described in the diagnostic analysis.
The new client application was used specifically to perform MFA requests on behalf of the Toolbox in the Train environment (Toolbox MFA flows relied on this client).

ADFS and ENABLEJWT: ENABLEJWT is a client registration parameter within Active Directory Federation Services (ADFS) that, when set for a client, causes ADFS to issue JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) to that client. The parameter directly controls ADFS token-issuance behavior; it was enabled on the problematic client and subsequently adjusted.

ADFS: REQUESTMFAFROMCLAIMSPROVIDERS is an ADFS-specific client registration parameter (client setting) that instructs ADFS to forward multi‑factor authentication (MFA) requests to the configured claims providers, effectively delegating MFA handling to those claims providers. The setting was enabled on the new client and was identified as contributing to authentication failures.

ADFS and SAML: In federation scenarios ADFS may issue or rely on SAML as an alternative token format; when the ADFS ENABLEJWT option is not enabled, SAML is the referenced token type. Token-format compatibility between ADFS-issued tokens (SAML versus JWT) and target applications is therefore a key determinant of authentication success, and target systems must support the token format ADFS is configured to emit.

ADFS CONFIGURATION DOCUMENTATION records the corrected ADFS client registration parameters and configuration and explicitly ties those corrections to the ADFS configuration changes that were made to resolve the issue. The documentation directly documents the specific ADFS settings that were modified, including the corrected client registration parameters and the change details, creating an authoritative record of the remediation steps applied to ADFS.

The ADFS client registration is the configuration object stored/used by ADFS for client behavior; ADFS enforces the registration parameters (e.g., EnableJWT, RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) defined in the client registration.

ADFS and MFA: ADFS is the authority that orchestrates and enforces multi-factor authentication (MFA) for registered clients; the client registration parameters in ADFS determine how MFA is applied, delegated, or forwarded for each client (relying party). Misconfigured MFA-related parameters on ADFS altered delegation/forwarding of MFA requirements, caused login failures, and were identified and corrected as part of the root-cause analysis and remediation.
ENABLEJWT causes ADFS to issue JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) to client applications; thus ENABLEJWT directly enables JWT usage in the authentication flow.
ENABLEJWT is a parameter contained within the ADFS client registration; the registration had EnableJWT set to true on the new client.
The new client application had ENABLEJWT set to true in its ADFS client registration, causing ADFS to issue JWTs to this client and contributing to the authentication failures.

When REQUESTMFAFROMCLAIMSPROVIDERS is enabled, MFA execution and prompts are delegated to configured claims providers (external identity/MFA providers), creating a direct operational link between the ADFS setting and those providers.

Forwarding MFA to external claims providers increases complexity in authentication flows and therefore has implications for logging, monitoring, and auditing of authentication events (noted in the analysis).
REQUESTMFAFROMCLAIMSPROVIDERS is a parameter contained within the ADFS client registration; the registration had this flag set to true on the new client causing delegation of MFA.
The new client application had REQUESTMFAFROMCLAIMSPROVIDERS set to true in its ADFS client registration, causing MFA to be forwarded to external claims providers for MFA processing.
REQUESTMFAFROMCLAIMSPROVIDERS modifies MFA handling by instructing ADFS to forward MFA prompts to external claims providers, directly altering how MFA is executed for the client.
MFA execution can be performed by configured claims providers when delegation is enabled; claims providers act as the external MFA executors referenced by the analysis.

source: KF25: SHAREPOINT – EXTERNAL PROJECT MEMBERS LOSE ACCESS SPORADICALLY (TI-1583327)
description: As part of initial troubleshooting hypotheses, token/cache behavior (cached access tokens or token validity) was considered as a potential cause of intermittent access problems; tests (full logout/login) rejected this hypothesis in favor of a synchronization/group-object issue.

External guest users are accounts present in the Customer Company 25 AG tenant (Entra ID) as Guest users and are members of cloud group constructs used to grant SharePoint access; they are external to the On‑Prem AD and thus were excluded when the On‑Prem group object was evaluated.

Employee 31 escalated the ticket to the BU Infrastructure – Identity team (Assigned Group changed) to perform in‑depth identity/synchronization analysis; indicates operational handover from initial assignee to the identity team.
System Engineer 14 took the ticket for in‑depth analysis as part of or assigned from the BU Infrastructure – Identity team.
System Engineer 14 reviewed Azure AD Connect behavior as part of the root-cause analysis and used its synchronization context to reproduce and explain the issue.
System Engineer 14 used Microsoft Graph queries to review group membership and compare Cloud-native and synchronized On‑Prem group members.

System Engineer 14 inspected SharePoint behavior (folder permissions and which group Object ID SharePoint referenced), reproduced access loss in test by re-synchronizing the On‑Prem group, and used SharePoint evaluation behavior (Object ID vs. name) to determine root cause.
The SharePoint service hosts the folder /Projects/Compliance_Review_2025 where access incidents were reported; folder permissions were investigated relative to group membership.

HELPDESK inspected SHAREPOINT permission settings as part of first-line troubleshooting, reviewing unique permissions and the permission group SP_Compliance_Main, performing folder-permission and group-membership checks directly in SHAREPOINT and running test access checks to reproduce and characterize the issue. Those checks showed that SP_Compliance_Main contains the group COMPLIANCE_TEAM and that external users were members of COMPLIANCE_TEAM, a finding that likely explains the observed access behavior.
The SharePoint-associated group SP_Compliance_Main was found to contain or reference the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group; investigators examined membership mappings between these constructs.

SharePoint was observed to reference/evaluate the synchronized On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM object (by Object ID) for effective permissions in at least one scenario, which caused external guests to be excluded when the On‑Prem group was referenced.

The Cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group contained external guest users; these memberships existed only in the cloud group and not in the On‑Prem group, which is why those external users lost access when the synchronized group object was used by SharePoint.

Azure AD Connect synchronization gradually overwrote or deactivated the Cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM object (same display name but different Object ID), causing SharePoint to evaluate the synchronized On‑Prem object instead of the original cloud object and leading to access loss for external guests.
Microsoft Graph was used to enumerate members of the Cloud-native group to confirm which external guests were present there.

The cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group exists in Entra ID/Azure AD and originally contained external guest members; it is hosted and managed in the cloud directory (source = Azure AD) until synchronization/overwrite occurred.

The synchronized On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group object contained internal users managed On‑Prem; because external guests were not present On‑Prem, they were not included when the On‑Prem object was evaluated.
Azure AD Connect synchronized the On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group into the cloud; this synchronization changed which group object (Object ID) SharePoint ultimately referenced.
Microsoft Graph was used to enumerate members of the synchronized On‑Prem group to confirm that it contained primarily internal users and excluded cloud-only external guests.

The COMPLIANCE_TEAM (On‑Prem) group object exists within the On‑Prem Active Directory (Windows Server AD) and contains internal user members; this object was synchronized to the cloud and has a different Object ID than the cloud-native group of the same name.

Entra ID contained the external guest accounts; investigators observed, via the Entra ID portal, that affected users had differing "source" attributes (some showed "source = Windows Server AD" for affected accounts vs "source = Azure AD" for functioning externals), aiding root-cause identification.

Azure AD Connect synchronizes objects from the On‑Prem Windows Server AD (domain controllers) into the cloud directory; the On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group originated here and was synchronized, contributing to the conflict with the cloud-native group.

Azure AD Connect synchronizes On‑Prem AD objects into Entra ID / Azure AD (cloud directory). The synchronization target is Entra ID where cloud groups and guest accounts reside; Azure AD Connect's behavior (overwriting/deactivating the cloud group) caused the access issues observed in Entra ID/SharePoint.

target: BLUEPRINT: SECURE WEB PUBLISHING – KF40 – VERSION 3.2

External DNS resolution for PORTAL.KF40.CH was tested and found correct, indicating DNS was not the primary issue; nevertheless the FQDN was the path that exposed the WAF/header problem.

The WAF/reverse proxy fronts traffic to the backend application server; it is responsible for TLS inspection/termination and header forwarding. When TLS inspection was active, the X-Forwarded-Proto header required by the application server was not forwarded, causing the backend-signaling mismatch and 502 errors. The blueprint mandates header forwarding for TLS offload scenarios.

TLS certificate was installed (customer reported) and WAF/TLS configuration is relevant to certificate binding and TLS termination. The WAF is expected (per blueprint) to perform TLS termination and present correct signaling to the backend; certificate validity was checked and found OK.

target: BLUEPRINT: SECURE WEB PUBLISHING – KF40 – VERSION 3.2
description: The WAF/reverse proxy configuration was compared to and corrected according to the blueprint. The blueprint prescribes TLS termination on WAF, custom header rewrite rules, exceptions, and templates required for the portal; the actual WAF config deviated from this and was adjusted to match the blueprint.

A recent firewall security patch/update caused templates/policies (inspection template assignment) to be reloaded/overwritten, which resulted in the WAF loading "Default-Strict" and losing the custom "Portal-App-Template" / rewrite rule. This change in the network/security stack led to the observed header removal and 502 outcomes until the template and custom rules were restored.

The reverse proxy/WAF is responsible for forwarding or rewriting the X-Forwarded-Proto header to the backend when performing TLS offload/inspection; the header was missing when TLS inspection was active, causing the observed failures.

After the firewall/security patch the default inspection/profile "Default-Strict" was reloaded/activated on the reverse proxy/WAF, replacing the custom Portal-App-Template and altering header handling.
The Portal-App-Template is the custom WAF/inspection template required for the portal; it was not active after the patch and had to be restored/assigned on the WAF to meet blueprint requirements.

The WAF should host a custom header rewrite rule to ensure X-Forwarded-Proto is forwarded; this rule was missing and its absence directly contributed to the 502 errors until the rule/config was restored.

TLS inspection is an active function of the WAF/reverse proxy in this environment; tests show enabling inspection removed/failed to forward headers and produced 502s, indicating inspection behavior needed correct template/rules.

The HTTP profile applied on the WAF controlled header handling; ticket notes the active HTTP profile did not match the documented/profiled version required by the blueprint and needed to be corrected.
Firewall configuration includes NAT and port 443 rules for inbound HTTPS traffic; helpdesk verified these were present and correct during initial checks.

The firewall/security patch caused the default "Default-Strict" template/profile to be reloaded on the security stack, which resulted in the WAF using Default-Strict instead of the Portal-App-Template; the patch indirectly caused configuration drift.

The load balancer performed health checks and marked the backend application server as "Healthy," indicating the application server was functional and that the 502 errors originated in the reverse-proxy/WAF/inspection path rather than backend failure.

The load balancer performed health checks and marked the backend server as "Healthy," indicating the backend application server was operational and that the error originated in the publishing/inspection layer rather than the backend itself.

The custom header rewrite rule ensures the backend/application server receives X-Forwarded-Proto: https as required by the application; without this rewrite the backend did not detect original HTTPS and returned errors.

When TLS inspection was active, the application server stopped receiving the expected HTTPS signaling (X-Forwarded-Proto) and returned 502; disabling inspection restored header forwarding and login functionality, demonstrating a direct impact on backend behavior.

target: BLUEPRINT – MICROSOFT TEAMS DIRECT ROUTING & SESSION HANDLING – VERSION 2.1
description: The ticket investigation referenced the blueprint; the final remediation aligned Basel's NAT/session settings with the blueprint's recommendations and the ticket closure cites the architecture-based adjustment.
Microsoft Teams Direct Routing relies on an SBC for SIP signaling/media to the PSTN; the SBC's behavior (BYE at ~30s) was central to the failure analysis.

Direct Routing logs contain signaling and session data related to Teams external calls and were analyzed to confirm that signaling established correctly but sessions were being terminated after a short interval.

target: END USER DEVICES (DOCKING STATION / LAN CABLE / OTHER DEVICE)
description: Device-level tests (different device, LAN vs docking station) showed the problem persisted across endpoints, indicating the cause was network/session configuration rather than endpoint-specific Teams client faults.
The SBC logs recorded the call flow (INVITE/200 OK and a BYE after ~30s) and were the primary log source for diagnosing the premature disconnects.
SIP messages are exchanged and processed by the SBC; the observed BYE originated from the SBC and terminated calls after session expiration.

target: BLUEPRINT – MICROSOFT TEAMS DIRECT ROUTING & SESSION HANDLING – VERSION 2.1
description: The blueprint contains guidance on Direct Routing and SBC/session handling; adjustments to SBC/session parameters were validated against the blueprint recommendations.

Temporary SBC parameter changes were applied during tests to demonstrate that altering session handling prevented the disconnects, confirming the session/NAT timeout root cause before permanent changes were made.
SIP session behavior is governed by the Session-Expires / session timer settings in SIP headers; the header values (30s vs 180s) directly influenced session lifetime and refresh behavior.
The BYE observed in the SBC logs is the SIP message form that ended the call; it was the specific SIP transaction seen after call establishment (INVITE / 200 OK).
The effective session lifetime was impacted by the NAT/UDP session timeouts applied to the Voice VLAN; a 30s NAT timeout caused sessions to be dropped despite SIP timers observed in the signaling.

SIP Session Timers are the protocol-level mechanism negotiated via the Session-Expires header; the investigation reviewed these timers (30s vs 180s) to understand session lifetimes and refresh expectations.

Firewall analysis showed no blocking and standard ports open, but firewall/NAT behavior (session timeouts) in conjunction with the Voice VLAN's NAT policy was implicated in premature session expiration.
VoIP traffic was placed onto the new "Voice" VLAN at the Basel location; that VLAN's distinct NAT policy and timeout settings directly affected VoIP session persistence.

The blueprint is authored for and applies to Customer Company 40; it describes how that organization's web applications must be published securely via the perimeter architecture.

The blueprint defines the WAF role and operational rules (TLS termination, inspection, header rewrite, templates) and mandates validation of WAF profiles and policies after patches.
The blueprint requires validation of WAF templates, inspection policies, and TLS profiles after firmware/security updates and mandates that any changes to WAF profiles or reverse proxy rules be validated against the document.
The blueprint explicitly forbids use of generic "Default-Strict" WAF profiles for productive portal services and requires dedicated templates instead.
The blueprint prescribes how the customer portal (portal.kf40.ch) must be published (via the WAF and Portal-App-Template) and lists it as a covered application.

target: INTERNAL WEB SERVICES WITH EXTERNAL INTERFACE
description: The blueprint covers internal web services that expose external interfaces and mandates they be published through the WAF/Reverse Proxy with required protections rather than direct exposure.

The blueprint includes validation steps for certificates and TLS profiles (decision tree item "Was a certificate recently renewed?") and thus expects coordination with the certificate service for renewal impacts.
The WAF performs TLS termination at the perimeter (TLS offloading) as specified by the blueprint; TLS is terminated on the WAF before requests are forwarded to backends.

The WAF must use the Portal-App-Template for portal applications; the template contains header rewrite rules, session handling, HTTPS semantics preservation, and HTTP/2 compatibility required by the blueprint.
Header rewrite rules are implemented on the WAF to preserve/adjust host and protocol headers for backend interpretation and to avoid redirect loops or broken authentication flows.
The WAF provides or integrates with load balancing to distribute incoming requests across backend application servers; load balancing must conform to session persistence requirements.

The WAF must set and preserve X-Forwarded headers (e.g., X-Forwarded-Proto: https and X-Forwarded-Host) and must not remove or overwrite them through inspection/security profiles so backends receive original request context.

The WAF and reverse proxy layer operate together at the perimeter: the WAF handles security (inspection, TLS termination, template enforcement) while the reverse proxy handles forwarding, host header handling and session persistence.
The Perimeter Zone is the logical location where the WAF is deployed and where TLS termination, inspection policies, and template enforcement occur as specified by the blueprint.

The WAF depends on certificates and TLS profiles managed by the certificate service for TLS termination; certificate renewal or misconfiguration at the WAF can disrupt external access and is explicitly checked in the blueprint decision tree.

WAF profiles (templates) are deployed on the WAF and determine behavior for inspection, header handling, TLS settings, and session management; blueprint mandates using dedicated templates like Portal-App-Template for productive portals.
Inspection policies are applied by the WAF to inspect and potentially modify traffic; these policies must be validated after updates to ensure they haven't cleared custom rewrite rules or headers.
The reverse proxy layer forwards client requests to backend application servers and must preserve or convey original HTTPS semantics (via headers) so backends can process requests correctly.

The reverse proxy layer implements session persistence/session handling to keep user sessions routed consistently to the correct backend instance, supporting authentication flows and stateful interactions.
The reverse proxy operates in the Perimeter Zone to forward external requests to internal backends and handle host header/session persistence; it co-locates with the WAF at the perimeter.
Reverse proxy/host header handling relies on correct DNS records to route requests to the appropriate virtual host/backend; mismatch between DNS and host-header rules can break access.

Header rewrite rules are implemented in the reverse proxy/WAF to preserve original-request context (e.g., X-Forwarded headers) and to avoid redirect loops between HTTP and HTTPS; they are critical for correct backend behavior.
The Portal-App-Template contains documented header rewrite rules that preserve HTTPS semantics and handle authentication flows; blueprint requires these rules to remain intact after updates.
The Portal-App-Template includes session handling configuration to ensure authentication flows and session affinity behave correctly for published portal applications.

The Portal-App-Template is an example of a WAF profile/template that must be applied for portal applications; WAF profiles include templates such as Portal-App-Template and the blueprint forbids using generic profiles like Default-Strict in production.
Backend application servers are located in a separate server VLAN to avoid direct Internet exposure as required by the blueprint.
Backends rely on X-Forwarded headers to correctly interpret the original request scheme and host because TLS is terminated at the perimeter and internal transport is HTTP by default.
Backend application servers are hosted within the Internal Application Zone and rely on preserved HTTPS semantics and header context from the perimeter to function correctly.
The Internal Application Zone is realized as one or more server VLANs where backend application servers reside to prevent direct Internet exposure.

Header rewrite rules implement and preserve X-Forwarded headers (and other header adjustments) so backends receive accurate original-request information and to prevent redirect loops between HTTP and HTTPS.
Header rewrite rules are the mechanism used to set and preserve X-Forwarded headers so backends can interpret the original HTTPS request despite TLS termination at the perimeter.

DNS service resolves the customer portal hostname (portal.kf40.ch) to perimeter addresses so clients can reach the published portal; DNS affects routing and must align with reverse proxy/WAF configuration.
External client traffic originates from the Internet and is terminated/inspected in the Perimeter Zone (WAF/Reverse Proxy) before being forwarded to internal services.
WAF profiles encapsulate or reference specific inspection policies; changes to profiles can alter which inspection policies are active, so both must be validated after updates.

description: Helpdesk triaged whether the issue appeared on the smartphone as well as the laptop, linking the smartphone as a test vector in the incident investigation.
Contact Person 11 confirmed the issue occurred only on the laptop (not on smartphone) and participated in remote checks that verified settings on that device.
Contact Person 11 used the portal to report/confirm the issue and to receive communications about resolution.
Employee 35 followed the Blueprint checklist (Teams notification settings, Windows Notification Center, Focus Assist, Teams restart) during remote troubleshooting and remediation.
Employee 35 is the assigned ticket owner/engineer and is part of (or working for) the Consulting Sample Company that executed the troubleshooting and issued the closure communication.
Teams relies on its notification channel (banner/toast) to show pop-up alerts; the banner channel was disabled in the user's Teams settings which prevented desktop pop-ups.
Teams desktop notifications flow through the Windows notification subsystem; checks of the Windows Notification Center were part of the Blueprint to ensure OS-level delivery is enabled.
Microsoft Teams runs on the user's laptop and its client settings (banner notifications) were directly relevant to whether pop-up notifications were displayed on that device.

Even if Teams notification channel is enabled, Windows Focus Assist can suppress or block pop-ups; in this incident both Teams banner was disabled and Focus Assist was active, combining to block notifications.
Focus Assist is an OS feature active on the user's laptop; its enabled state on the laptop blocked Teams pop-ups until adjusted.
Focus Assist is a Windows OS feature; the active Focus Assist setting on the user's Windows laptop suppressed Teams pop-up notifications in this incident.
The Windows Notification Center on the user's laptop is the OS component that surfaces app notifications; it was checked as part of troubleshooting per the Blueprint.
Windows Notification Center is a component of the Windows OS; it was checked during troubleshooting to verify OS-level notification delivery.
The recorded Windows Update activity was performed on the affected laptop (the endpoint where notifications were blocked), linking update activity to the device under investigation.
A recent Windows Update had run on the user's laptop the week before the incident and was considered as part of triage when assessing causes for changed notification behavior.
The ticket TI-1569482 was opened on behalf of Customer Company 25 AG and concerns resources and users within that organization.
Contact Person 03 reported the access problem and is the customer point of contact for the ticket.
Employee 18 was the assigned owner/engineer for the ticket and carried out investigation, remediation, testing and closure actions.
The Helpdesk received the initial report and performed early triage and communications recorded in the ticket activity log.
The incident described in TI-1569482 is specifically about users receiving "Access denied" when attempting to access this SharePoint folder; the folder is the direct target of the ticket.
Consulting Sample Company executed the troubleshooting and remediation actions recorded in the ticket and issued the closure communication confirming the root cause and access restoration.
Employee 18 performed the Entra ID review (inspected group membership and nesting), made the nesting correction in Entra ID, and monitored synchronization and access results.

Through testing and direct membership changes (adding the user directly to SP_Project_IT) Employee 18 verified that direct group membership granted immediate access to the folder and used that to validate the root cause before fixing nesting.
Helpdesk triaged and/or escalated the incident to Employee 18 (assigned owner) who carried out the technical investigation and remediation.
Employee 18 performed the test measure by adding the test user directly to SP_Project_IT to validate that direct membership granted access and to demonstrate the nesting problem.
Employee 18 corrected the group nesting in Entra ID by properly integrating IT-Projekte into the new SP_Project_IT group (administrative action in Entra ID that restored effective permissions).
The folder resides in SharePoint Online; SharePoint Online was reachable during troubleshooting and its permission view was used to determine which group provided access.

SharePoint's permission view showed that access to the folder was granted via the group SP_Project_IT (users should obtain permissions through membership/nesting into that group).
SP_Project_IT is the permission group referenced by SharePoint as granting access to the folder; correct membership/nesting into this group is required for users to access the folder.
Entra ID group membership and nesting determine effective SharePoint permissions for the folder (the root cause involved Entra ID group nesting, demonstrating Entra ID's role in access control).
The working/comparison user had functioning access (used to compare with affected users who received "Access denied"); this contrast helped identify membership differences as the cause.
After direct assignment to SP_Project_IT, the test user immediately had access to the folder, demonstrating that group membership in SP_Project_IT conveys access to the folder.
SP_Project_IT is an Azure AD (Entra ID) security group created to manage access to the SharePoint folder; group lifecycle and membership are managed in Entra ID.
SP_Project_IT_v1 was an existing/deactivated Azure AD group within Entra ID; it was deactivated/removed as part of cleanup after the new group was introduced and nesting corrected.
Prior to remediation, the IT-Projekte group remained nested in the old/deactivated group SP_Project_IT_v1 (incorrect nesting), so users did not inherit permissions from the new SP_Project_IT group.
After corrective action, IT-Projekte was nested correctly into the new SP_Project_IT group, which restored effective permissions and resolved the access issues.
The test user was added directly to the SP_Project_IT group as a diagnostic measure (direct membership granted immediately effective permissions).
The troubleshooting notes state that the working user is a member of SP_Project_IT_Lead (membership used as a comparison to diagnose inconsistent group assignments).

description:
The decision tree and use cases are part of the blueprint content and provide stepwise troubleshooting and validation procedures (e.g., check federation configuration, presence settings, recent policy changes, domain allow lists).
The Decision Tree & Use Cases are a contained section of the blueprint document and provide procedural steps for validating and troubleshooting federation, presence and external access scenarios as referenced in the main document.
