source: MICROSOFT TEAMS FEDERATION & EXTERNAL ACCESS – CUSTOMER COMPANY 52
description: The blueprint explicitly references and must align with the organization's Security Policies KF52; those policies constrain acceptable federation and external-access configurations and require blueprint validation prior to changes.
Internal users in Tenant KF52 connect to and use the Teams Cloud Service for chat, presence and calls per the architecture defined in the blueprint.
The Federation Layer mediates SIP-based federation and presence exchange between KF52 and External Tenants; external tenants are the federation peers for cross-tenant communication.
When domain restrictions are enabled, an External Tenant must be present in ALLOWEDDOMAINS to allow federation between KF52 and that external tenant.
PRESENCE EXCHANGE transmits presence state information to and from External Tenants, enabling external visibility (subject to privacy and policy settings).
Chat messages are exchanged between Tenant KF52 and EXTERNAL TENANT instances over the federation channel mediated by the Federation Layer and governed by federation/external access policies.
Call signaling (SIP federation) is exchanged between Tenant KF52 and EXTERNAL TENANT for cross-tenant call setup and control as part of federation.
Teams Cloud Service routes cross-tenant communication through the Federation Layer (logical/functional component) where SIP federation, presence exchange and policy evaluation occur.
The Teams Cloud Service enforces presence sharing modes and therefore directly affects how presence is displayed to external tenants and how call signaling/chat availability behave across federation.
The Teams Cloud Service executes tenant policy evaluation as part of request processing, applying configured messaging/external access/conditional access rules before allowing federation actions.
SIP federation is the signaling/protocol mechanism used by the Federation Layer to perform cross-tenant call signaling and presence exchange.
The Federation Layer performs and routes presence exchange between Tenant KF52 and External Tenants; presence exchange is a core function of that layer in the blueprint architecture.
The Federation Layer mediates cross-tenant chat delivery and applies federation rules/policies to chat traffic between Tenant KF52 and External Tenants.
The Federation Layer handles SIP federation and call signaling for cross-tenant calls; call setup/teardown and signaling are routed through this layer.
ALLOWFEDERATEDUSERS is a specific parameter of the federation configuration that enables or disables federation with federated tenant users.
ALLOWPUBLICUSERS is a federation configuration parameter controlling whether public (non-tenant) users are allowed to interact with the tenant.
ALLOWEDDOMAINS is the configuration list used by federation configuration when domain restrictions are enabled; partner domains must be explicitly listed here to permit federation.
Changes in Teams Messaging Policies can indirectly affect federation/chat behaviors and are listed as dependent configurations to validate against the blueprint when altering federation settings.
External Access Policies are tightly related to federation configuration and control cross-tenant access; changes here must be validated according to the blueprint.
Conditional Access Policies may influence federation flows and presence visibility and are identified as a policy dependency to consider when diagnosing federation issues.

Tenant federation configuration is the tenant-specific instantiation of the federation configuration model described in the blueprint and must be validated against the document's rules before changes are applied.
Domain restrictions are a mode/option within the federation configuration model and directly influence whether ALLOWEDDOMAINS filtering is applied to incoming/outgoing federation traffic.

Security Policies KF52 inform and constrain the permitted federation configuration (e.g., whether public users are allowed, whether certain domains may be federated) and require blueprint validation for changes.
Domain restrictions use the ALLOWEDDOMAINS list as the control point — when DOMAIN RESTRICTIONS are enabled, partner domains must be present in ALLOWEDDOMAINS to permit federation.

ALLOWEDDOMAINS contains the PARTNER DOMAINS (external domain names) that are permitted for federation when domain restrictions are active; membership here enables cross-tenant communication for those domains.
ENABLEPRESENCEPRIVACYMODE is the presence-related flag within Presence Sharing that, when enabled, changes external visibility of presence (privacy behavior).
PRESENCESHARINGSETTING configures the specific mode (Full, Limited, Disabled) in which Presence Sharing operates and directly controls external presence behavior.

Presence Sharing operates in these modes; the selected mode determines whether external presence is fully visible, limited (e.g., shown offline) or disabled entirely, affecting presence display and availability synchronization.
Privacy Mode settings are a configurable subset of Presence Sharing that control whether presence privacy behavior is active and therefore directly impact external presence display and signaling.

Presence Sharing settings (EnablePresencePrivacyMode, PresenceSharingSetting) determine the resulting PRESENCE DISPLAY visible to external tenants and thereby control whether external users see Full, Limited or Disabled presence for KF52 users.
The support ticket TI-1622483 was opened on behalf of Customer Company 60 AG and concerns a printer at that customer's Basel location.
Contact Person 15 reported the issue and interacted with support, providing the initial report and later confirming the printer was functioning again.
Employee 21 is the assigned ticket owner who performed analysis, coordinated remediation, and closed the ticket.
Consulting Sample Company executed the support work and issued the closure communication to the customer; the company is the service provider for this ticket.
The incident described in TI-1622483 specifically concerns the MFP-BASEL-01 printer which was not responding to print jobs.
The ticket resolution referenced and used the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard; configuration changes were implemented according to this standard and the closure note cites adherence to it.
Contact Person 15 reported that MFP-BASEL-01 was not processing print jobs and later confirmed the device was functioning after remediation.
Employee 21 coordinated internally with the Network Team to confirm the firewall rule omission and to have the rule adjusted to restore connectivity.
Employee 21 performed remote access and analysis on the Print Server, observed job/queue behavior, and executed tests to isolate the issue before escalating/coordination.
MFP-BASEL-01 was moved into the separate Printer VLAN during a recent change; this VLAN placement is central to the reachability/segmentation issue.
After the firewall rule was adjusted, the MFP-BASEL-01 became reachable from the Print Server and user workstations, restoring printing capability.
User workstations submit print jobs to the Print Server for delivery to MFP-BASEL-01; user-side test prints were performed to validate functionality after the fix.

The printer (MFP-BASEL-01) was reachable from clients on the local Basel network (successful ping from a client within the same location network), indicating the printer was accessible to local devices even when the server network could not reach it.

The Print Spooler service runs on the Print Server and manages the Print Queue; the service was observed running during analysis indicating the delivery problem was network-related rather than a stopped spooler.
The Print Queue resides on the Print Server and temporarily holds jobs; jobs appeared then disappeared from this queue, evidencing failed delivery to the printer.
The Print Server (on a different server network) could not reach devices in the Printer VLAN due to segmentation and missing routing/firewall allowances, causing print failures.
The firewall rule controlled traffic between the Print Server and the Printer VLAN; its initial configuration prevented successful ping/print attempts from the server to the printer.
The Print Server is hosted on the server network segment; its inability to reach the printer indicated the server network lacked access to the Printer VLAN.
The Printer VLAN represents the new network segment introduced for devices at the Basel location; the VLAN is the implementation of that network segment.

The standard documents the Location Concept that printers may be placed in a separate VLAN but must remain reachable by the print server; this guidance directly relates to the Printer VLAN configuration.
There was network segmentation between the server network and the Printer VLAN that prevented communication; this segmentation was the root cause until routing/firewall rules were adjusted.
Routing must be configured to permit traffic between the Print Server's network and the Printer VLAN; the standard explicitly requires routers/routes to allow server-to-printer reachability.
The Network Team reviewed and adjusted the firewall rule that had been blocking access from the server network to the Printer VLAN, enabling required communication.
The firewall rule that was initially configured prevented traffic from the server network to the Printer VLAN; adjusting the rule allowed the server network to reach the printers.
Client workstations are connected to the Basel location network; a client on this network was able to ping the printer, demonstrating client-side reachability within the location network.

Routing configuration (and associated firewall rules) between the server network and other VLANs determined whether the Print Server could communicate with printers; absent or incorrect routing contributed to the outage.
Helpdesk performed initial triage and troubleshooting steps recorded in the ticket (questions, client checks, cache clear/reinstall) before escalation.
The ticket was escalated to BU Unified Communications for specialist Direct Routing/media flow analysis.
Multiple headsets were tested and showed the same symptom across devices, which indicated the problem was not headset-specific but network/infrastructure-related.
After configuration adjustment, a 48-hour monitoring period recorded no anomalies and supported ticket closure confirming stability of the remediation.
Microsoft 365 service status was checked and reported normal during troubleshooting, which narrowed the problem to local/network infrastructure rather than a global Microsoft outage.

System Engineer 18 or the engineering team analyzed logs and determined the firewall port restriction at Zurich was causing media failure and then coordinated/apply configuration change per architecture to restore media flow.

target: BLUEPRINT – MICROSOFT TEAMS DIRECT ROUTING & MEDIA FLOW – VERSION 2.1
description: System Engineer 18 used the Blueprint as authoritative guidance to implement the correct configuration for media ports/behavior at the Zurich site.
Helpdesk performed internet and packet-loss checks and found no packet loss, helping rule out general network instability and focus on firewall configuration.
Microsoft Teams is part of the Microsoft 365 platform; Teams functional status and diagnostics were investigated within the Microsoft 365/Teams operational context during the incident.
The SBC is the Direct Routing element that connects Teams to PSTN and was analyzed (status online, SIP signaling present) to determine where media flow failed.

target: BLUEPRINT – MICROSOFT TEAMS DIRECT ROUTING & MEDIA FLOW – VERSION 2.1
description: The Blueprint documents Direct Routing media flow requirements (Media Bypass behavior, RTP port requirements, NAT traversal, location-specific firewall rules) and was used to compare expected configuration against the Zurich site.
Media Bypass is a Direct Routing mode described in the Blueprint where client media travels directly to the SBC, making client firewall/NAT port allowances critical.
SBC logs showed Microsoft-originating RTP streams were visible, but RTP from internal clients to SBC was not consistently visible, indicating asymmetric media flow impacting audio.
SBC logs demonstrated SIP signaling (INVITE/200 OK) was correct, indicating call setup succeeded and the problem was at media/RTP layer rather than signaling.
Teams Admin Center showed SBC status was Online and SIP signaling was successful; used as a diagnostic source to confirm signaling health.
SBC logs showed successful SDP/media negotiation, indicating the codec/port negotiation step completed even though client RTP packets were not consistently arriving at the SBC.
SBC logs showed an RTP stream originating from a Microsoft IP (the Microsoft media side), indicating Microsoft-side media was visible and reachable to the SBC during diagnostics.

Temporarily opening the wider UDP port range (including dynamic RTP ports) immediately restored bidirectional RTP/audio in tests, proving that RTP flow was blocked by the restrictive firewall rule when ports were closed.
Teams Admin Center analysis showed SIP signaling was functioning correctly for PSTN calls, helping isolate the issue to media/RTP.

SDP is exchanged as part of SIP signaling to negotiate media parameters; SIP signaling (INVITE/200 OK) succeeded and SDP negotiation was logged as successful, helping isolate the fault to actual RTP packet flow rather than negotiation.
A firewall rule change was applied at the Zurich location during a security-hardening weekend change, creating the outbound port restriction that caused the issue.

target: FIREWALL RULE – OUTBOUND PORT RESTRICTION 3478–3481 ONLY
description: This firewall rule is the specific configuration change implemented on the Zurich firewall and is the proximate cause of blocked RTP media from clients.
Firewall logs were reviewed and revealed the new outbound restriction rule and supported correlation between the change and the incident start time.

source: FIREWALL RULE – OUTBOUND PORT RESTRICTION 3478–3481 ONLY
description: The firewall rule allowed only the STUN/TURN ports 3478–3481 and blocked the dynamic RTP port range 49152–53247 required for client-to-SBC media when Media Bypass is used; mismatch caused media failure.

source: FIREWALL RULE – OUTBOUND PORT RESTRICTION 3478–3481 ONLY
description: Temporarily opening the wider UDP port range (including dynamic RTP ports) during testing immediately restored bidirectional audio; reapplying the restrictive rule removed audio again, functionally proving the firewall rule was root cause.

source: FIREWALL RULE – OUTBOUND PORT RESTRICTION 3478–3481 ONLY
description: The security-hardening change at the Zurich site caused/implemented the restrictive firewall rule that limited outbound UDP to ports 3478–3481, which blocked dynamic RTP ports and led to one-way audio.

source: FIREWALL RULE – OUTBOUND PORT RESTRICTION 3478–3481 ONLY
description: The firewall rule permitted STUN/TURN/ICE ports (3478–3481) but blocked the dynamic RTP range; this allowed some discovery/ICE signaling while preventing the client RTP media from being established to the SBC when Media Bypass was used.
STUN/TURN/ICE operations use UDP 3478–3481 while Teams RTP uses dynamic ports (49152–53247); both port sets are documented as required in the Blueprint for full media connectivity.

source: BLUEPRINT – MICROSOFT TEAMS DIRECT ROUTING & MEDIA FLOW – VERSION 2.1
description: The Blueprint documents NAT traversal considerations and requirements; NAT behavior and traversal affect how Media Bypass and direct RTP streams function and were part of the architectural rationale for required firewall/NAT configuration.
Zurich location was configured to use Media Bypass, which caused client RTP to be sent directly to the SBC and made the outbound port restriction impactful at that site.
NAT traversal and NAT mappings are significant when Media Bypass is enabled because clients must be able to send RTP directly to the SBC and NAT behavior can affect RTP reachability and port mapping.
The security-hardening change was applied at the Zurich location over the weekend and is temporally correlated with the start of the reported audio problems affecting users there.

source: SHAREPOINT PERMISSION & GROUP ARCHITECTURE – CUSTOMER COMPANY 25
description: The Architecture Overview is a design subsection of the blueprint that explains the permission propagation flow and structural rationale for the group model and permission model.

Entra ID is the system where group nesting is defined and maintained; SharePoint evaluates effective permissions from that nesting. Incorrect nesting in Entra ID causes missing effective permissions in SharePoint.
Azure AD groups exist within Entra ID (the document treats Azure AD groups and Entra ID as the identity constructs and directory where group membership/nesting is authoritative).
Documented group nesting is an operational requirement recorded in Entra ID, since SharePoint derives effective permissions from nesting held in Entra ID/Azure AD.

This policy mandates that Azure AD groups (SharePoint resource groups) are the only objects to which SharePoint permissions may be assigned; ties the policy to the directory construct AZURE AD GROUPS (Entra ID).
The example shows the functional group IT-Projects is nested into the resource group SP_Project_IT, meaning members of IT-Projects gain permissions indirectly via SP_Project_IT.
Users are members of functional groups (e.g., an individual user is a member of IT-Projects) — this is the primary membership relation used to derive access.
Finance-Team is an example functional group that must be nested into a dedicated SharePoint resource group to receive permissions for SharePoint resources; illustrates model separation requirement.
Risk-Team is another example functional group that must be nested into resource groups rather than being granted direct permissions in SharePoint; part of the same model.
The example shows SP_Project_IT_Lead is nested into or references SP_Project_IT, illustrating resource-group nesting relationships that affect effective permissions.
SP_Project_IT is presented as the authorized group for the example folder /Projects/2025_Migration; folder permissions are assigned to the resource group rather than to users.
User gains effective access to the project folder only indirectly: User -> Functional Group -> Resource Group -> Folder. The document enforces no direct user permissions at folder/library level.
After group changes, the document requires waiting for synchronization/propagation and verification of effective permissions on folders such as /Projects/2025_Migration to ensure access is correct.

Effective permissions on the project folder are derived from the current group nesting (functional groups nested into resource groups) — incorrect nesting yields missing access as noted in the document's "MUST BE OBSERVED" section.
Naming and deactivation rules are part of the group's lifecycle controls and must be followed to ensure correct synchronization and propagation of membership/permissions.

Synchronization and propagation requirements operationalize the lifecycle process by instructing administrators to wait for directory/SharePoint sync and then verify effective permissions after group changes.
Unique Permissions are a specific operational constraint within the Permission Model dictating preconditions and documentation required before creating unique ACLs on folders or libraries.
The policy forbidding direct user permissions is a primary rule within the Permission Model and enforced by the document's guidance and operational procedures.
Documented group nesting is a required practice within the Group Model to maintain clear relationships between functional groups and resource groups and to avoid incorrect effective permissions.
The prohibition of parallel/historical groups is operationalized through naming and deactivation rules which require cleanup and removal of old versions before new group activation.

source: MANAGEMENT REPORTING & FINANCIAL REPORTING INFRASTRUCTURE STANDARD V1.0
description: The document contains a decision tree and use cases that map specific symptoms to component checks (e.g., Data Mart reset, SQL connection, AOS service, IIS configuration, cipher-suite/TLS checks); this section prescribes the troubleshooting workflow for the described components.

AOS servers handle authentication and communications for the Financial Reporting Service; AOS acts as the request broker so the Financial Reporting Service depends on healthy AOS instances for proper operation.
The Financial Reporting Service hosts the Report Designer and the rendering engine — the Report Designer depends on this service to open, render and edit reports.
The Financial Reporting Service consumes aggregated data from the Data Mart for rendering reports; Data Mart availability and consistency directly affect report generation and rendering performance.
The Report Rendering Engine is a hosted component of the Financial Reporting Service and performs the actual rendering of reports; it runs within or alongside the Financial Reporting Service runtime.

Misconfigured TLS or disallowed cipher suites can cause Report Designer to start slowly, fail to authenticate, show unknown authors, or fail to load reports. The document explicitly links TLS/Cipher compatibility to Report Designer behavior.

When report authors appear as “unknown,” the document lists checking the service account connection as a troubleshooting step — service account authentication/permissions directly affect Report Designer metadata and author attribution.
Network latency can cause the Report Designer to start extremely slowly or delay interactions; the decision tree includes network latency checks when the Report Designer performance is poor.
Service accounts are used to access the Data Mart and reporting metadata; service account connectivity and permissions affect Data Mart access and thus report availability.
The Data Mart is implemented on the SQL backend; Data Mart operations (aggregations, consistency) depend on SQL connectivity and the underlying database health.
The decision tree recommends checking Data Mart reset and consistency when issues are isolated to one environment or when reports are missing, indicating a direct troubleshooting relationship.
A Data Mart reset is an operation performed on the Financial Reporting Data Mart to recover from data inconsistency; the document recommends this step when issues are isolated to one environment.
Users interact with the reporting system via browser or Report Designer; these client requests are handled by AOS servers which manage authentication and communication to backend reporting services.

The standard requires synchronized cipher-suite changes; after TLS/Cipher Suite adjustments, a restart of AOS servers is required, indicating a direct operational dependency between TLS settings and AOS server behavior.

TLS policy changes affect AOS servers (they participate in authenticated communication); the document requires AOS restarts following TLS/cipher adjustments, indicating a direct operational dependency.
High network latency between users and AOS servers can degrade authentication and communication performance, contributing to slow report startup and rendering times.

The IIS / TLS layer implements TLS and cipher-suite settings; the IIS layer is responsible for enforcing the TLS configuration defined by the standard. Misconfigurations at this layer cause connectivity/authentication and performance issues.

When issues appear in multiple environments or TLS/cipher changes were made, the decision tree escalates checks to the IIS / TLS layer and cipher-suite configuration, showing the troubleshooting path to that component.

Cipher suites are implemented and enforced at the IIS / TLS layer; misconfigured or unsynchronized cipher-suite settings at this layer prevent successful TLS negotiation for clients and backend services.

Windows security updates can change available cryptographic libraries or TLS behavior, impacting TLS/cipher negotiation; the decision tree explicitly lists checking Windows updates as a troubleshooting step for multi-environment TLS issues.
TLS incompatibility can prevent successful metadata/authentication handshakes, resulting in the Report Designer reporting authors as “unknown”; the decision tree links TLS checks to this symptom.
When report authors appear as “unknown,” the document prescribes checking service account connections—service account authentication/permissions are a primary cause of missing author metadata.
The decision tree includes SQL connection checks as a primary troubleshooting step when reports or data are unavailable, linking the decision logic to SQL backend health.
Report metadata is stored and retrieved from the SQL backend/Data Mart; SQL connectivity and database health directly affect metadata availability and correctness.

target: BLUEPRINT – MICROSOFT TEAMS FEDERATION & EXTERNAL ACCESS – VERSION 1.4

ALLOWFEDERATEDUSERS is a specific flag/value in the tenant federation configuration that was observed as True and is part of the configuration state analyzed during troubleshooting.

COMPLIANCE_CORE is an Azure AD group defined in Azure AD / Entra ID and is the required group for label-enforced access/decryption of content protected by the "Confidential – Internal" sensitivity label.

The Sensitivity Label exposes the "Preserve custom permissions" option; this option was not enabled when the label was applied, resulting in the label not preserving the folder's existing ACL mapping.

Azure AD issues the access tokens that contain group/claim information (e.g., COMPLIANCE_TEAM) and are subject to caching/refresh behavior that affected access decisions.

Token caching behavior caused older/stale access tokens to continue permitting access temporarily (because they contained different claim state) until tokens were renewed — demonstrating how cached tokens led to intermittent allowed/blocked access.
The SharePoint folder's ACL referenced the Compliance_Core group (or had been assigned to that group), creating the mismatch when encryption referenced COMPLIANCE_TEAM instead.

The issue was reproduced in a test tenant by creating a folder with unique permissions and applying a Sensitivity Label afterwards to observe the same ACL vs. encryption mismatch and token timing behavior.

Contact Person 28 reported the issue and provided the initial symptom description that triggered the support ticket.

Employee 34 executed investigation steps recorded on the ticket (testing, log review, coordination, configuration adjustments) and validated successful delivery before closing the ticket.
Consulting Sample Company performed the troubleshooting, implemented changes per the Blueprint, coordinated with the mail team, and communicated resolution on the ticket closure.
The Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard was referenced and followed during the investigation and remediation; closure text explicitly states changes were made according to this standard.
The ticket describes failures of the Scan-to-Mail service for larger scanned documents; resolving the ticket required changes to both device scan profile and mail infrastructure limits.
Mail log review (SMTP relay and internal mail server logs) provided the primary diagnostic evidence used in the ticket's root-cause analysis and remediation plan.
Employee 34 performed device testing (one‑page and 25‑page scans), temporarily reduced resolution for tests, and adjusted the scan profile as part of remediation.

The Blueprint specifies Scan-to-Mail architecture and operational requirements (use of dedicated SMTP relay, aligning maximum file sizes with mail infrastructure, coordination for mail policy changes). The remediation followed these blueprint requirements.

The standard prescribes that maximum file size must be aligned with mail infrastructure and that changes to mail policies must be coordinated with scan systems; scan profile adjustments were made in accordance with the standard.
Scan-to-Mail sends messages through the dedicated SMTP relay; the relay's size limits directly affected whether scanned messages were accepted and delivered.

The SMTP relay enforced a maximum message size (documented as 10 MB during investigation). This configuration caused rejection entries ("Message size exceeds fixed limit") and prevented delivery of large scans.

SMTP relay logs contained the diagnostic entry "Message size exceeds fixed limit", which identified the relay as the rejection point; internal mail server logs showed no inbound message for large scans, corroborating relay-level rejection.

The scan profile determines message payload size; because the SMTP relay had a reduced maximum size, the scan profile needed adjustment to produce files within permitted limits (or the SMTP limit needed to be increased).

Messages accepted by the SMTP relay are forwarded to the internal mail server; because the relay rejected oversized messages, internal mail server logs showed no incoming messages for the failed scans.

target: SMTP RELAY LOG ENTRY: \"MESSAGE SIZE EXCEEDS FIXED LIMIT\"
description: This specific log entry originates from the SMTP relay and directly identifies the relay as the component rejecting oversized scan messages.

target: MAXIMUM EMAIL SIZE CHANGE (REDUCTION TO LIMIT SPAM)
description: The maximum email size change was a configuration on the SMTP relay (documented as 10 MB during the investigation) and was the immediate cause of oversized scan rejections.
The printer submits scanned messages to the SMTP relay for delivery; oversized messages sent by the device were rejected by the relay, preventing delivery to the internal mail server.

target: MAXIMUM EMAIL SIZE CHANGE (REDUCTION TO LIMIT SPAM)
description: The mail team implemented the maximum email size reduction as an anti‑spam measure and later coordinated to moderately increase the limit during remediation.
Scan-to-Mail is implemented on the printer/scan device; the device produces the scanned PDFs and initiates SMTP delivery via the SMTP relay.

The printer's scan profile (600 dpi default, compression disabled) produced large PDFs for multi‑page scans; changing the profile (300 dpi and enabling compression) reduced file sizes and enabled successful delivery.

The "Send successful" confirmation is emitted by the printer/scan device after it submits the message to the SMTP relay, indicating device-side acceptance even when the relay later rejected the message due to size limits.

target: SMTP RELAY LOG ENTRY: \"MESSAGE SIZE EXCEEDS FIXED LIMIT\"

PDF compression is a configurable attribute of the scan profile; it was disabled initially and later enabled as part of the profile changes to reduce file size.
600 dpi was the default resolution setting within the scan profile and produced larger PDFs that exceeded the SMTP relay limit.
300 dpi was used as a temporary test configuration in the scan profile to reduce file size and verify successful delivery of larger multi‑page scans.

The support ticket TI-1616128 was created for an incident affecting an employee of Customer Company 52 AG and was recorded on behalf of that organization.
Contact Person 07 reported the issue by telephone and provided symptom details that initiated the ticket.
Employee 33 was assigned as the ticket owner, performed troubleshooting (remote session), validated the fix, and closed the ticket.
Consulting Sample Company executed the support activity, communicated the resolution, and signed the closure message for the ticket.
Helpdesk performed initial triage questions (device type, test call) recorded in the ticket activities and contributed to the troubleshooting workflow.
The incident described in TI-1616128 specifically affected Microsoft Teams calls (users could hear others but were not heard), making Teams the application context of the ticket.
The unnamed affected employee is the subject of support ticket TI-1616128 (the user who could not be heard during Teams calls).
Contact Person 07 is the reporting representative for Customer Company 52 AG and provided details on behalf of that organization.
The affected employee is an employee of Customer Company 52 AG and the incident was reported on their behalf by Contact Person 07.
Employee 33 investigated Teams settings, observed the selected input device in Teams, executed a test call, and validated that Teams audio transmission worked after the hardware fix.
During a remote session Employee 33 inspected the user's USB headset, discovered the microphone cable was not fully inserted, and reconnected it to restore functionality.
Employee 33 used the Windows Sound menu to verify the absence and subsequent restoration of the input signal while troubleshooting.
Employee 33 established/used the remote session to perform hands-on troubleshooting on the affected employee's workstation and verify audio signals.
Employee 33 executed or observed the Teams test call during troubleshooting to validate microphone operation before and after reconnecting the headset microphone cable.

Teams displayed "USB Audio Device" as the selected microphone input. Although selected, it produced no input level until the hardware issue was fixed, linking the Teams selection to the physical USB device.
The microphone is the required audio input for Teams calls; because it produced no input, Teams could not transmit the user's voice to call participants until the mic signal was restored.

MICROSOFT TEAMS / CAMERA: Microsoft Teams requires the camera to be allowed by OS and app permissions for video. During the incident Teams’ video and chat were functioning correctly, demonstrating the application’s video path was operational; however, a specific camera device was not selectable in Teams because OS/app-level permissions were blocking it. Taken together, this shows the issue was isolated to audio input rather than a complete endpoint or video failure — i.e., a permissions restriction prevented selection of one camera device, but did not prevent Teams from delivering video and chat.
The affected employee used Microsoft Teams for calls where the audio-input issue occurred (could hear others but was not heard).
The Teams Test Call is a diagnostic feature of Microsoft Teams used to validate microphone input and call audio behavior during troubleshooting.
The USB headset contains the microphone used for voice capture; the headset's mic was the input device that failed to transmit audio until repaired.
The headset's microphone cable was part of the headset assembly and was found to be not fully connected; reseating the cable restored headset microphone functionality.

The USB headset was connected to the user's Windows workstation and selected as the input device in Teams; the workstation's OS initially showed no input from the headset until the mic cable was reseated.
The headset is represented in the OS/Teams as the "USB Audio Device" selection; Teams displayed this device as the selected microphone input.
The microphone cable is the physical connection for the headset mic; it was not fully inserted which prevented the microphone from producing any input signal.

Windows Sound menu showed no input level from the microphone while the cable was disconnected and showed activity immediately after reconnection, indicating OS-level lack of input caused the Teams audio failure.
The same USB audio device selected in Teams showed no input signal in the Windows Sound menu, confirming the issue was present at the OS/device level rather than Teams-only.
The Windows Sound menu is part of the user's Windows workstation OS and was used to observe the absence and subsequent restoration of the microphone input signal.
The remote session connected into the user's Windows workstation, allowing inspection of Teams settings and the Windows Sound menu to observe input levels.
The affected employee performed or participated in Teams test calls as part of the troubleshooting workflow; test calls initially failed and later succeeded after the hardware reconnection.
TI-1571046 was opened on behalf of Customer Company 25 AG and describes access issues affecting that company's users and SharePoint resources.
Contact Person 05 reported the problem and was the ticket contact for communications and closure notifications.
Employee 21 was assigned as the ticket owner and performed analysis, policy adjustments, device sync, and closure actions documented in the ticket.
The ticket was created via the customer portal and portal notes/status changes were used to track the incident lifecycle.

The incident concerns access to SharePoint and specifically the missing /Projects/ERP_Upgrade/Finance folder for some users; SharePoint permissions and access behavior were central to troubleshooting.
Consulting Sample Company performed the investigation and remediation actions documented in TI-1571046 and communicated the resolution to the customer (signed the closure).
Ticket notes call out that users with MFA enabled reported the issue more frequently, indicating MFA presence correlated with the observed access problems in this incident.

The ticket documented that some users accessed from private devices and these access patterns contributed to inconsistent access and to Conditional Access evaluation differences reported in the incident.

target: CONDITIONAL ACCESS POLICY – REQUIRE COMPLIANT DEVICE FOR FINANCE RESOURCES
description: Employee 21 reviewed and adjusted the Conditional Access policy (excluded the SharePoint project folder from the Finance-specific CA rule) as part of remediation steps documented in the ticket.
Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization for affected hybrid devices to update compliance state faster as part of remediation.
Employee 21 documented that users with an activated MFA requirement reported the issue more often, indicating Employee 21 observed and recorded MFA as a notable correlation during the investigation.
SharePoint permissions are assigned to the SP_ERP_Project group (project-level access); the folder access behavior was evaluated relative to this group membership.

target: CONDITIONAL ACCESS POLICY – REQUIRE COMPLIANT DEVICE FOR FINANCE RESOURCES
description: The Conditional Access policy applied to finance resources (including the SharePoint project/folder); policy evaluation impacted whether users were allowed to access the SharePoint folder. The policy was adjusted to exclude the folder as part of remediation.

Tokens used to access SharePoint were sometimes issued before group membership changes and thus contained outdated claims; cached tokens allowed some users to continue with old claims while others saw access changes immediately.

The /Projects/ERP_Upgrade/Finance resource is a folder/resource within the SharePoint service and was the specific item not visible to some users; SharePoint permission assignments determine visibility of this folder.
Azure Sign-In Logs are produced by Entra ID and were reviewed to analyze Conditional Access outcomes and token issuance timing relevant to the incident.
Entra ID issued the authentication tokens whose timing and cached reuse led to inconsistent access; token claims and issuance timing are attributes of Entra ID operations.
The ERP_Finance group is a member of the SP_ERP_Project group (group nesting), which was reviewed during analysis (group nesting itself was not the root cause).

/Projects/ERP_Upgrade/Finance visibility is controlled via membership/permissions assigned to the SP_ERP_Project group; the ticket confirms the folder relied on that group structure for access control.

source: CONDITIONAL ACCESS POLICY – REQUIRE COMPLIANT DEVICE FOR FINANCE RESOURCES
description: Hybrid-joined devices had stale/unknown compliance status which caused the Conditional Access policy to evaluate as "not satisfied" for some sign-ins, preventing access until device compliance was updated.

source: CONDITIONAL ACCESS POLICY – REQUIRE COMPLIANT DEVICE FOR FINANCE RESOURCES
description: Users accessing from private devices were subject to the "Require compliant device for Finance Resources" Conditional Access policy and, because private devices were often non-compliant/unknown, CA evaluation failed for those sign-ins.

source: CONDITIONAL ACCESS POLICY – REQUIRE COMPLIANT DEVICE FOR FINANCE RESOURCES
description: Conditional Access policy evaluation uses device compliance status as a condition; when device compliance status is "unknown" or stale, the CA policy evaluated as "not satisfied", blocking access until compliance was updated.

Sign-in logs recorded events showing both "Conditional Access not satisfied" and successful token issuance for SharePoint Root, providing evidence that tokens were issued at times that led to inconsistent access due to CA timing and caching.

MFA requirements can affect authentication flows and token issuance; users with MFA may experience additional interactive steps or different token lifetimes, correlating with token caching/outdated claims issues observed in the ticket.

Sign-out from Office clients was required to clear cached tokens; the remediation "User fully logged out (Office + browser)" demonstrates that Office sessions retained stale tokens which prevented updated claims from taking effect until sign-out.
Browser session tokens and caches were cleared during troubleshooting ("Browser cache cleared") to remove stale authentication tokens so new tokens containing current group/claims could be obtained.

Device synchronization pushes device state/compliance to Entra ID; faulty/delayed synchronization for hybrid-joined devices caused compliance status to remain outdated for 6–8 hours and contributed to the access problem.

Device compliance status is an attribute of hybrid Azure AD joined devices. The ticket identified that hybrid-joined devices had faulty/delayed synchronization so compliance status remained outdated ("only updated after 6–8 hours"), impacting access.
Private/personal devices are more likely to report non-compliant or "unknown" device status in Entra ID, which was observed in the incident and contributed to CA evaluation failures.
The ticket was opened on behalf of Customer Company 60 AG; the incident concerns a resource and user within that customer's environment.
Contact Person 08 reported the problem by email and confirmed the outcome after remediation.
Employee 15 was the assigned owner/engineer who investigated and resolved the ticket, performed gpupdate /force and validated printer mapping and test print.
The support ticket describes the printer not being visible for the affected user — the printer is the object of the incident.
Consulting Sample Company performed the troubleshooting and remediation actions recorded in the ticket and communicated the closure to the customer.
Contact Person 08 is the customer-side reporter and primary contact at Customer Company 60 AG who opened the ticket and confirmed resolution on behalf of that organization.
Contact Person 08 reported the issue on behalf of (or about) the new employee whose account lacked the printer mapping; they acted as the customer-side reporter/liaison for that user.
Employee 15 checked the GPO status, determined the GPO for printer assignment had not been applied, and updated/forced Group Policy application (gpupdate /force) to remediate the issue.

Employee 15 inspected the user's account membership, executed remediation steps (gpupdate /force and user re-login), and validated that the user's account then received the printer mapping and could print.

Employee 15 is the support engineer assigned to the ticket and is acting as a representative/engineer of Consulting Sample Company (the consulting organisation that signed the closure and performed the remediation).

Employee 15 investigated Group Policy processing status and executed the remediation (forced Group Policy update), thereby causing the Group Policies to apply and the printer assignment to be delivered to the user session.
The GPO is responsible for assigning the location-based printer to user accounts; failure of the GPO to apply prevented the printer from being mapped for the affected user.
After Group Policy processing, the user's workstation had the network/location-based printer automatically mapped and was able to send a test print to the printer device/resource.

The GPO uses AD group membership (location-based AD groups) to determine which printers to assign to users; thus AD group membership and GPO processing together produce the automatic mapping behavior.

The specific GPO for printer assignment is one of the Group Policy objects within the organization's Group Policies framework; the GPO is the policy that controls automatic mapping of location-based printers to users.

The user's workstation is the endpoint that applies the printer-assignment GPO during policy processing; after the GPO was applied (gpupdate /force and re-login), the workstation received the printer mapping.
The affected user account is a member of the AD group used for printer assignment; membership was verified during troubleshooting and is required for GPO-based mapping.
The affected user's account is an Active Directory account; AD stores the user's identity and group memberships which are used during Group Policy evaluation and printer assignment.

The AD group for location-based printers is defined and stored in Active Directory; AD group membership is evaluated by Group Policy processing to determine which printers are assigned to which users.

source: ACTIVE DIRECTORY DOMAIN CONTROLLER & MICROSOFT DEFENDER FOR IDENTITY STANDARD V1.0
description: The blueprint/standard document is authored for and applies to Customer Company 10 environments and DCs (document scope/target).
Defender sensors run directly on Domain Controllers to capture authentication/LDAP/replication telemetry; DC health and sensor behavior are tightly coupled.

Windows Firewall is host‑based on the Domain Controller and must be configured to allow sensor outbound connections and necessary internal AD ports; misconfigurations block sensor function and AD flows.
These internal AD ports and protocols must remain available on Domain Controllers for AD authentication, replication and for accurate sensor monitoring of AD traffic.
Domain Controllers (on‑prem and Azure) are infrastructure owned and operated within Customer Company 10; the document prescribes standards for those DCs.
Sensors send telemetry to the Defender Cloud Service over outbound HTTPS; the cloud service receives and analyzes telemetry for security alerts and health status.
Sensor connectivity to the Defender Cloud Service requires outbound HTTPS (TCP 443); blocked egress on this port causes sensor connection errors and “unhealthy” status.
Sensors require DNS resolution of Microsoft endpoints; DNS failures prevent endpoint resolution and telemetry delivery, causing sensor health degradation.
Local firewall rules on the DC can block the sensor's network traffic; document requires Windows Firewall be configured per Microsoft recommendations.
Perimeter firewall must permit outbound HTTPS to Defender endpoints; changes or blocking rules at the perimeter can impact all sensors and create multi‑DC incidents.
For Azure DCs, NSG rules control egress; a restrictive NSG can prevent sensor communication with the cloud service and cause unhealthy status.
Proxy configuration or proxy authentication requirements can prevent sensors from establishing outbound HTTPS connections unless proxy is correctly configured or proxied endpoints are whitelisted.
TLS or certificate problems can prevent secure HTTPS connections between sensors and Defender cloud endpoints, causing intermittent or persistent connectivity failures.
Operational state (service running or requiring reinstall) is a primary troubleshooting step; if inconsistent installation or service failures exist, reinstallation and monitoring are recommended.
The document's decision tree outlines specific checks for sensor health (service running, outbound connectivity, DNS, event logs, proxy/TLS, agent version) to diagnose sensor unhealthy states.
Defender sensors are installed directly on on‑prem Domain Controllers to capture authentication and replication telemetry; sensor operation is tied to DC health.
Defender sensors run on Azure‑hosted DCs as on‑prem DCs do; they require correct cloud network/security configuration to send telemetry.
Sensor and system event logs capture Defender sensor errors, service state, and network issues that indicate why telemetry or health checks fail.
Sensor telemetry feeds the health/alerting pipeline; unhealthy sensors lead to missing or delayed health alerts.

Internal security software running on DCs or inline in the network can block sensor traffic or otherwise interfere with telemetry; the document lists checking for internal security software as part of troubleshooting persistent issues.
After agent reinstallation, sensors must reestablish connectivity to the cloud service; the document instructs monitoring cloud connectivity/health after reinstall.
The cloud service status and availability are reported via the Defender Service Health Portal; portal checks help differentiate service provider incidents from customer network/configuration issues.
Health and security alerts are generated and surfaced by the Defender Cloud Service based on telemetry from sensors; when sensors cannot reach the cloud service, alerts may be missing.
On‑prem Domain Controllers rely on the host Windows Firewall to allow required sensor and AD traffic; misconfigured host firewall rules on on‑prem DCs can block telemetry and AD protocols.
Host firewall rules on DCs must align with perimeter rules and Microsoft recommendations; misalignment leads to blocked sensor traffic even if perimeter rules are correct.
Perimeter firewall policies determine whether outbound HTTPS is allowed; blocking this port interrupts sensor-to-cloud communication.
Changes in network segmentation or topology can change the effective scope of perimeter firewall rules and NSGs, potentially blocking sensor traffic; the document requires review of such changes.
Firewall rules are implemented at the perimeter firewall; changes or restrictive rules here often affect outbound HTTPS and cause multi‑DC connectivity problems.
Azure NSG rules can block or allow outbound 443 for Azure DCs, directly affecting sensor connectivity to Defender cloud endpoints.
Azure Domain Controllers' network traffic is controlled by Azure NSG rules; incorrect NSG configuration can block outbound 443 or Microsoft endpoints and prevent sensor connectivity.
TLS/certificate validity and TLS policy affect the success of outbound HTTPS connections; certificate/TLS mismatches lead to failed egress despite port availability.
On‑prem DCs must keep internal AD ports open and unchanged for AD functionality and for sensors to monitor authentication/replication traffic.
Azure DCs also require internal AD ports to remain available for AD replication and authentication monitoring.

The decision tree recommends consulting the Defender Service Health Portal as part of troubleshooting to determine whether detected issues stem from Microsoft service outages versus local/network problems.

The decision tree explicitly instructs checking event logs (sensor/network errors) as part of diagnosing single‑DC or multi‑DC issues; event logs are a primary diagnostic source referenced by the document.

source: PRINT & SCANNING INFRASTRUCTURE STANDARD – CUSTOMER COMPANY
description: The decision tree is a component/section of the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and operationalizes the document's troubleshooting logic and use cases.
Print servers host printer queues and are the exclusive network-facing access point for printers; printers are addressed through print server queues rather than direct client connections.
User devices send print jobs to the central print server rather than connecting directly to printers; GPO-provided mappings on user devices point to print server queues.
Print servers create and host location-based printer queues which are then assigned to users/devices based on location or department.
Standardized, approved drivers are installed/configured on print servers and must be validated after OS updates to maintain correct mappings and function.
Printer provisioning creates queues on the central Windows print server; provisioning actions are executed on the print server and recorded as queues.
Network-level reachability between the print server and printers (or between client and print server) is validated by VLAN/ping tests to ensure packet delivery for print jobs.
Printers are deployed in a dedicated printer VLAN as required by the standard (VLAN-separated printer networks), isolating printer traffic and centralizing network topology for printers.
Printer assignment can be controlled via Active Directory groups as part of the location/department assignment model described in the standard.
Device Manager on user devices is used to confirm the presence of local printer/scanner hardware (driver/hardware visibility), which helps distinguish client-side issues from server/queue issues.
DNS resolution of printer or print-server hostnames is a documented troubleshooting step; DNS failures can prevent clients from resolving and reaching printers or print servers.

Print Spooler operation (on server and/or clients where relevant) directly affects the ability to process and forward jobs to printers; spooler failures can cause jobs to remain in queues and block printing.
Scan-to-Mail is performed by multifunction printers (scan-capable devices) that generate the email payload sent through the SMTP relay; the multifunction device is the source of scanned messages.
GPOs perform automatic assignment of location-based printer queues to user devices according to location or departmental membership.
The Print Spooler service manages job queuing and dispatch for print queues; spooler health is a troubleshooting step when jobs are stuck in the queue.
Print jobs are submitted into location-based printer queues on the print server; the queue is the logical container for jobs destined to a particular printer.
Default printers for a location are implemented via location-based printer queues and applied automatically to users/devices as described in the document.
Point-and-Print restrictions are implemented and managed via Group Policy; the document instructs verification of these settings especially after Windows updates.
Provisioning enforces the Driver Standard by configuring standardized/approved driver versions on the created queues to ensure compatibility and supportability.

Printers reside in a separate VLAN and clients do not access printers directly across networks; network/VLAN reachability affects print job delivery and is part of the troubleshooting flow (Ping/VLAN checks).
Successful delivery of print jobs often requires network/VLAN-level reachability to the physical printer (or appropriate routing to the print server), per the decision tree checks.
VLAN reachability checks validate routing and ACLs for the dedicated printer VLAN where printers reside; failure indicates network segmentation or routing issues affecting printing.
Network reachability from the user device to the print server or printer VLAN is verified via ping/VLAN checks as part of client-to-server connectivity diagnostics.
The decision tree directs investigators to check the Print Spooler service (A3) when jobs are stuck in the queue; spooler health is a core remediation step.
The Print Spooler service manages queued print jobs and is responsible for dispatching them to printers; spooler faults can cause jobs to remain stuck.
A paused print queue prevents the spooler from dispatching jobs; diagnosing a paused queue often leads to checking spooler state and queue configuration.
The decision tree includes verifying DNS resolution (A4) for printer hostnames/print server names as part of connectivity troubleshooting.

Scan-to-Mail submissions authenticate to the dedicated SMTP relay using a configured service account; incorrect credentials will prevent delivery.
Service account credentials are required to authenticate to the SMTP relay; changes to mail server authentication or account credentials will affect Scan-to-Mail functionality.
The SMTP relay is secured with TLS per the standard; TLS configuration and certificate/trust issues are a potential cause for Scan-to-Mail failures and must be validated.
The SMTP relay/mail server maintains a mail queue for outgoing scan emails; checking the mail queue is a diagnostic step when emails are not delivered.
Scan-to-Mail deliveries are relayed via the dedicated SMTP relay described in the standard; the relay is the transport/service used to send scanned emails to recipients.
Scan-to-Mail authentication to the SMTP relay relies on a configured service account; incorrect credentials or account changes can break scan delivery.
Scan-to-Mail traffic to the SMTP relay is secured with TLS per the standard; TLS/certificate configuration issues can prevent successful relay connections.
Default printer assignments may be tied to AD group membership to implement location- or department-based defaults for users.
Mail being held, dropped or delayed can be caused by spam/filtering rules; the decision tree instructs checking spam/filter rules when a scan is not delivered despite queueing.
If Scan-to-Mail appears to succeed but messages do not arrive, the mail queue on the SMTP relay or mail server should be inspected as directed by the decision tree (B2).
Spam filtering or mail rules may intercept or drop scanned emails; decision tree advises checking spam/filter rules when delivery is missing though mails are queued.
The decision tree uses the presence or absence of a print job in the queue (A2/A3) to route diagnostics toward client-side checks or server/queue checks.
The decision tree instructs checking whether a print queue is paused (A3) when jobs remain stuck; paused queue is a direct diagnostic branch.
The decision tree includes VLAN reachability and ping checks (A3/A4) to validate network connectivity between print server and printers or clients and printers.
